title: C++ authoring uses one first-party engine without LuaSnip
summary: Structured templates, dollar expressions, suggestions, context gates, include edits, and tabstop navigation have one first-party owner; Neovim's built-in snippet primitive tracks placeholders.
details: LuaSnip, cmp_luasnip, and friendly-snippets are not dependencies of this configuration.
The first-party catalog and interaction policy are custom, but placeholder/mirror tracking delegates to vim.snippet instead of reimplementing a generic extmark session engine.
Structured templates expand explicitly on Space or Tab; rule-of-three/five templates no longer fire automatically while a token is still being typed.
An unresolved or context-invalid dollar expression is preserved with the requested space rather than deleted.
nvim-cmp remains an optional LSP/path/buffer completion surface and queries the authoring engine's live-block state instead of duplicating its grammar.
rationale: The previous split between LuaSnip autosnippets, LuaSnip tabstop templates, a custom dollar parser, and duplicated cmp gating created competing state and brittle timing.
The project needs C++-specific context and structured edits, not the maintenance surface of two general-purpose snippet engines.

Neovim's built-in LSP snippet implementation already supplies tested tabstop selection and mirror synchronization, so rebuilding that low-level mechanism would add failure modes without adding domain behavior.
alternatives: Retain LuaSnip only for tabstop templates; rejected because it preserves a second catalog/session owner for little benefit.
Implement placeholder extmarks and mirror propagation from scratch; rejected because the built-in primitive meets the required subset.
Use only plain text substitutions; rejected because mirrors and deterministic field navigation are important for extensive templates.

provenance.note: Owner asked whether LuaSnip should be removed in favor of a broader custom authoring engine.
Current catalog/session policy and completion integration.

evidence.claim: Real mappings, built-in mirrors, context protection, grammar behavior, include insertion, and non-destructive failures are covered.
No LuaSnip-related plugin or completion source remains.

questions: Does this configuration still use LuaSnip?
Who owns C++ templates, dollar suggestions, and tabstop sessions?
What happens when a dollar shorthand is mistyped?

last_verified.method: Dependency audit plus isolated real-insert interaction tests.
